summary: ContactRollupService_Naive.cls hits four canonical SOQL anti-patterns at LDV scale (expected ~2M Account rows). The query-in-loop on Contact escalates to P0 because it executes once per Account row and will hit the 100-SOQL limit before the 101st parent. The select-* on Account, missing-LIMIT on the top-level query, and missing security mode each compound the cost. Recommended rewrite lifts the inner query out into a single bulk SOQL call keyed on a Set<AccountId>, projects only the fields actually used downstream, adds WITH USER_MODE, and routes the call through a ContactSelector that extends BaseSelector.
confidence_rationale: Source is fully inlined and the four anti-patterns are unambiguous; MEDIUM (not HIGH) because the harness lacks a live Query Plan Tool probe to confirm the rewrite hits a custom index on Contact.AccountId.

findings.title: SOQL inside a for-loop on Account
findings.detail: Inner Contact query runs once per Account; at the expected 2M-record scale this will exhaust the 100-SOQL governor limit before the 101st parent. The query also re-fetches data already keyed by AccountId.
findings.recommendation: Lift the query out: `Map<Id, List<Contact>> contactsByAccount = new Map<Id, List<Contact>>(); for (Contact c : [SELECT Id, AccountId, Email FROM Contact WHERE AccountId IN :accountIds WITH USER_MODE]) { contactsByAccount.computeIfAbsent(c.AccountId, k -> new List<Contact>()).add(c); }` — then iterate the parent list once.

findings.title: Select-* on Account
findings.detail: Query reads every field on Account. At 2M rows this fetches ~25× more bytes than needed, increases heap pressure, and slows the query plan.
findings.recommendation: Project only the fields used downstream: `SELECT Id, Name, AnnualRevenue FROM Account WHERE ...`. Codify via an `AccountSelector` that exposes one method per use case.

findings.title: Top-level Account query has no LIMIT
findings.detail: At LDV scale a query without a LIMIT can return up to 50,000 rows in a single SOQL or, if invoked from a Queueable, time out. Even with the rewrite above, the agent must not unconditionally pull every Account.
findings.recommendation: Either add a WHERE clause that bounds the set (e.g. `LastModifiedDate = LAST_N_DAYS:1`), wrap the work in a Batch Apex job sized to chunks of 200, or add an explicit `LIMIT 50000` and document the rationale.

findings.title: Both queries lack WITH SECURITY_ENFORCED / WITH USER_MODE
findings.detail: Account and Contact queries read fields without enforcing the running user's FLS — the rollup may include fields the running user cannot legally see and surface them downstream.
findings.recommendation: Add `WITH USER_MODE` (preferred on API 60+) to both queries. If a system-mode pass is required, isolate it in a `without sharing` selector method and document the reason.

deliverables.title: Rewrite — bulkified ContactRollupService
Why the rewrite is bulkification, not a micro-optimization
deliverables.content:
# ContactRollupService rewrite — rationale

**Before** (anti-pattern composite): N+1 SOQL queries, select-*, no LIMIT, no FLS.
**After**: 2 bulk queries (bounded by the input Set), narrow projection, USER_MODE, AggregateResult for the count instead of materializing rows.

## What the rewrite buys
- **SOQL count**: O(N) → 2 (regardless of input size).
- **Heap**: O(N × Contact-row-size) → O(N × 16 bytes) for the count map.
- **FLS posture**: bypasses → enforces.

## What it does NOT buy
- The rewrite still depends on `Contact.AccountId` being a custom-indexed FK (it is, by default). At 2M Accounts × an average of 5 Contacts each, the second query reads ~10M Contact rows — confirm the org's index posture via the Query Plan Tool before scheduling this in a Batch.

## Follow-up
- **`apex-refactorer`** — the rewrite belongs in a `ContactSelector` extending `templates/apex/BaseSelector.cls`; this class should consume the selector, not own the query.
- **`security-scanner`** — re-scan after the rewrite to confirm no other security smells (sharing keyword, etc.) remain.

process_observations.observation: At the stated scale (~2M Accounts), the original code will hit the SOQL governor limit on the 101st loop iteration — the bug surfaces as a runtime exception only at the volume reported in inputs, not in dev sandbox testing.
Rewrite belongs behind a Selector that extends BaseSelector so other classes can reuse the same FLS-aware query.
process_observations.followup_reason: because the bulk query and the Account/Contact projection are reusable across multiple call sites and belong in a selector layer, not in this service class.

citations.used_for: Bulkification rewrite, AggregateResult substitution for count work, and the LIMIT/projection guidance at LDV.
WITH USER_MODE preference over WITH SECURITY_ENFORCED on API 60+.
Reference scaffold for the recommended ContactSelector extraction.

followups.because: because the bulk query belongs in a ContactSelector extending BaseSelector; refactoring is out of scope for this agent.
because the rewrite changes the SOQL security mode and a follow-up scan should confirm no new sharing-keyword or CRUD/FLS gap was introduced.
